---
title: "2.6 Comparing Boolean Expressions"
subtitle: "Selection and Iteration"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## De Morgan's Laws

### Move the NOT inside and flip the connector

![De Morgan's Laws](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-6-comparing-boolean-expressions/demorgan.png){fig-align="center" width="48%"}

The goal is to show that two different-looking Boolean expressions can be logically equivalent.

::: {.tight-list}
- `!(a && b)` is equivalent to `!a || !b`
- `!(a || b)` is equivalent to `!a && !b`
:::

## Flipping Relational Operators

### Moving `!` inward also changes comparison signs

::: {.tight-list}
- `!(c == d)` becomes `c != d`
- `!(c < d)` becomes `c >= d`
- `!(c > d)` becomes `c <= d`
:::

The classroom shortcut is: <span class="mark">move the NOT, flip the sign</span>.

## Simplifying Expressions

### Equivalent code can be easier to read

![Example simplification](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-6-comparing-boolean-expressions/demorganex.png){fig-align="center" width="52%"}

```java
!(x < 3 && y > 2)
```

can be simplified step by step into:

```java
x >= 3 || y <= 2
```

## Truth Tables and Equivalence

### Matching outputs prove two expressions are equivalent

Truth tables matter here because they let students compare:

::: {.tight-list}
- the original compound expression
- the negated version
- the simplified version
:::

If every row matches, the expressions are equivalent.

## Comparing Objects and Strings

### `equals` is usually the right comparison for object content

![String aliases](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-6-comparing-boolean-expressions/stringEquality.png){fig-align="center" width="34%"}

::: {.tight-list}
- objects can have many internal values
- `==` checks whether two references point to the same object
- `equals(...)` checks whether two objects should count as equal in content
:::

With strings, you almost always want `equals(...)`.

## String Equality Cases

### Same content is not always the same reference

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
![Alias case](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-6-comparing-boolean-expressions/s2ands3.jpg){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.soft-box}
Two useful cases:

- alias case: `s2 == s3` can be true
- new-string case: `s1 == s2` can be false even when `s1.equals(s2)` is true
:::
:::

![New strings with same characters](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-6-comparing-boolean-expressions/s1ands2.jpg){fig-align="center" width="40%"}

## Comparing with `null`

### Use short circuiting to stay safe

```java
if (s != null && s.indexOf("a") >= 0)
{
    System.out.println(s + " contains an a");
}
```

::: {.tight-list}
- `null` means no object is there
- calling a method on `null` causes a <span class="term">NullPointerException</span>
- `s != null && ...` is safe because of short circuit evaluation
:::

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- De Morgan transformation checks
- truth-table comparisons for equivalent conditions
- `==` versus `equals(...)` examples with strings
- `null` guard tracing with short-circuit logic
- <span class="term">2.6.8 Coding Challenge: Truth and Tracing Tables POGIL</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- apply De Morgan's Laws correctly
- flip relational operators when moving negation inward
- explain expression equivalence with truth-table reasoning
- distinguish `==` from `equals(...)` for strings
- explain why `obj != null && ...` is the safe pattern
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
